title: How child parts experience time differently than adult parts
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The clock in your chest doesn't tick the same way for everyone

metaDescription:
The clock in your chest doesn't tick the same way for everyone 

 I remember sitting in my therapist's office, completely baffled. She asked me how old I...
htmlContent:
The clock in your chest doesn't tick the same way for everyone

I remember sitting in my therapist's office, completely baffled. She asked me how old I felt in that moment, and I said "maybe seven or eight." I was thirty-four. My body was in a chair designed for adults. My hands were grown-up hands. But inside, something small was gripping the armrests like they were the only solid thing in a world that kept tilting.

Here's the thing. When you have complex trauma, time isn't a straight line. It's more like a spiral, or a series of trapdoors. You can be fine, totally fine, and then a sound, a smell, a tone of voice, and suddenly you're not in 2024 anymore. You're in 1987, or 1995, or whatever year your nervous system decided to bookmark as dangerous.

I've been thinking a lot about how child parts experience time differently than adult parts. Not just differently, but differently. Like they're living in a completely different dimension where the laws of physics don't apply the same way.

Stay with me here. This matters because if you don't understand how your child parts perceive time, you'll keep wondering why you can't just "move on" from something that happened decades ago. You'll keep blaming yourself for being stuck. And that blame? It's just another way your inner critic keeps the system frozen.

Why child parts don't know it's over

Bessel van der Kolk, the trauma researcher who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about how trauma gets encoded in the body without a timestamp. Your adult brain knows that the abuse happened thirty years ago. Your child part? It doesn't have access to that information. It never got the memo.

Think about it. When a child experiences something overwhelming, their brain hasn't fully developed the capacity for time perception. The prefrontal cortex, the part that understands past, present, and future, isn't fully online yet. So traumatic events get stored as now. Always now. Forever now.

I have a part that still believes my father is going to walk through the door at any moment. I'm forty-two years old. He lives in another state. We haven't spoken in seven years. But this part of me, this little girl part, she's still waiting. She's still bracing. She doesn't understand the calendar. She doesn't understand that I have a driver's license and a mortgage and a child of my own. She only understands that danger happened, and danger could happen again, and the only way to survive is to stay hypervigilant.

Janina Fisher, who wrote extensively about structural dissociation, describes this as a "failure of integration." The traumatic experience never gets woven into your life narrative. It stays in a separate compartment, frozen in amber. Your adult self moves forward, builds a career, falls in love, has children. But that child part is still in the same room, still smelling the same smells, still feeling the same terror.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Fisher's work on understanding how trauma fragments the self is a popular choice for people who want to understand why their parts don't age the same way they do.

Wild, right? You're reading this as a grown adult with responsibilities and bills and probably some gray hairs. But somewhere inside you, there's a part that still thinks it's five years old and helpless. And that part genuinely doesn't know that you survived.

The body doesn't check the calendar

I want to talk about what happens in the body when a child part gets activated. Because this is where the time distortion really shows itself.

When your nervous system detects a threat, real or perceived, it launches into survival mode. Your heart rate increases. Your breathing gets shallow. Your muscles tense. Your digestion shuts down. Cortisol floods your system. This is the stress response, and it's designed to be temporary. It's designed to help you fight, flee, or freeze, and then return to baseline.

But for people with complex trauma, the stress response doesn't always turn off. Or it turns on at the slightest trigger. And here's the problem: the body doesn't have a clock. It doesn't know that the threat is in the past. It only knows that it's feeling certain sensations, and those sensations mean danger.

So when a child part gets activated, your body literally goes back in time. Not in a metaphorical way. In a very real, physiological way. Your muscles remember how to hold the tension from when you were being hit. Your stomach remembers the nausea from when you were being screamed at. Your throat remembers the tightness from when you were trying not to cry.

I've had sessions where my body was shaking and my teeth were chattering, and my adult brain was saying "this is safe, this is a therapy office, there are pillows and tissues and a plant in the corner." But my body didn't believe me. My body was in 1993. My body was convinced I was about to be hurt.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets trapped in the body's incomplete survival responses. A child who couldn't run away, couldn't fight back, couldn't escape, their body never got to complete the action. So the energy stays locked in. And that energy doesn't age. It stays fresh, raw, immediate, like it happened five minutes ago.

This is why time alone doesn't heal trauma. Time passes, but the body doesn't update its internal calendar. The child part stays in that moment, waiting for someone to come back and finish the rescue.

What happens when adult parts and child parts clash over time

Here's where it gets really messy. You have an adult part that knows time is linear. It knows that events have a beginning, middle, and end. It knows that people grow up, that wounds scar over, that the past is over. This adult part has a fully developed prefrontal cortex. It can plan for the future. It can reflect on the past. It can say "that was then, this is now."

And then you have a child part that doesn't believe any of that. The child part experiences time as eternal. A minute of terror can feel like an hour. An hour of neglect can feel like a lifetime. And because the child part doesn't have the cognitive capacity to understand "this will end," every moment of suffering feels permanent.

So when these two parts try to coexist, you get a kind of internal war. The adult part says "just get over it, it's been twenty years." The child part says "it's happening right now, I can't breathe, help me." And you're stuck in the middle, feeling both things at once, unable to reconcile them.

This is where a lot of people get stuck in self-blame. They hear the adult part's voice more clearly, so they believe the child part is being dramatic, or weak, or manipulative. They try to force the child part to accept adult time. They say things like "you're safe now" without realizing that the child part doesn't have the neural wiring to understand safety in abstract terms.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work helped me understand that the recovery process isn't about convincing your child parts that time has passed. It's about helping them experience safety in their own timeframe, which might be very different from yours.

I've learned that I can't argue with my child parts about time. I can't show them a calendar and say "see, it's over." They don't care about calendars. They care about sensation. They care about connection. They care about being seen and heard in their own temporal reality.

How to start bridging the time gap

So what do we do with this information? How do we help our child parts experience time differently, or at least find some peace with the time they're stuck in?

First, we stop trying to drag them into adult time. That doesn't work. It just creates more conflict. Instead, we meet them where they are. If a part feels like it's five years old and terrified, we don't say "but you're forty, you have a car." We say "I see you're scared. I'm here now. I'm not leaving."

Second, we use the body to anchor ourselves. This is where the adult part can actually help. Your adult part has resources that the child part doesn't. It can feel the floor under your feet. It can notice the weight of your body in the chair. It can look around the room and see that you're in a different place. By grounding your body in the present, you create a container for the child part's experience. You're not denying their reality. You're just adding another layer to it.

Third, we practice what Deb Dana calls "glimmers." These are small moments of safety that we can offer to our child parts. A warm cup of tea. A soft blanket. A gentle voice. These things don't prove that the past is over. They prove that something different is possible right now. And over time, that difference starts to register in the nervous system.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on accepting our parts exactly as they are, without trying to fix them or rush them, has been invaluable for many survivors I know.

Fourth, and this is the hard one, we let the child part teach us about time. Because honestly? Child parts have something to offer. They know how to be fully present. They know how to feel things completely, without the adult brain's tendency to intellectualize and distance. They know that a moment can contain an entire universe of feeling. And in a world that constantly tells us to hurry up and move on, that kind of presence is actually a gift.

I'm not saying we should let child parts run our lives. That's not sustainable. But I am saying that the relationship between adult time and child time doesn't have to be a battle. It can be a dialogue. A dance. A negotiation between two different ways of being in the world.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's framework for understanding how developmental trauma affects our inner parts has helped countless people make sense of why they feel so young inside.

The truth is, I don't think my child parts will ever fully experience time the way I do as an adult. And maybe that's okay. Maybe the goal isn't to merge them into a single, unified timeline. Maybe the goal is to for both realities. To let the adult part plan for the future and reflect on the past, while letting the child part experience the raw immediacy of the present, even when that present is painful.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did something shift? Did a part of you relax a little, or tense up? That's the conversation happening. That's the negotiation between your different time zones.

Sit with that. Just for a moment. You don't have to figure it all out right now. You don't have to force your parts into agreement. You just have to notice that they're there, each in their own time, each doing their best to survive.

And maybe that's enough for today.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice
